matter.title: Grace C. Roe v. Summit Outfitters Co. et al.
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe hired Summit Outfitters Co. to build a custom single-family home for roughly $1363566, and now sues the general contractor, its excavation subcontractor Coastal Logistics Inc., and (by third-party complaint) the design professional Riverbend Analytics Inc. over framing and roof structural deficiencies the homeowner attributes to inadequate site drainage design.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe hired Summit Outfitters Co. to build a custom single-family home for roughly $1363566, and now sues the general contractor, its excavation subcontractor Coastal Logistics Inc., and (by third-party complaint) the design professional Riverbend Analytics Inc. over framing and roof structural deficiencies the homeowner attributes to inadequate site drainage design.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe contracted with Summit Outfitters Co., a general contractor, to construct a custom single-family home for a contract price of approximately $1363566. The work reached substantial completion on or about 2022-11-01. The project included a written 10-year structural warranty.

Beginning around 2024-07-12, Roe observed framing and roof structural deficiencies at the property. A subsequent investigation traced the problem to inadequate site drainage design, work that fell to the excavation subcontractor Coastal Logistics Inc. and that the homeowner contends departed from the plans prepared by the design professional Riverbend Analytics Inc.. The homeowner obtained a repair estimate of roughly $602549.

Grace filed suit in Sagadahoc County Superior Court against Summit Outfitters Co. for breach of the construction contract, breach of the implied warranty of workmanlike construction, and negligence, and against Coastal Logistics Inc. for negligent excavation and fill compaction. Summit Outfitters Co. answered, cross-claimed against Coastal Logistics Inc. for indemnity and contribution, and filed a third-party complaint impleading Riverbend Analytics Inc.. The matter is now in active, contested discovery.
undisputed_facts: Grace C. Roe and Summit Outfitters Co. entered a written construction contract for a custom single-family home.
The work reached substantial completion on or about 2022-11-01.
Roe reported framing and roof structural deficiencies at the property beginning around 2024-07-12.
disputed_facts: Whether the defects arose from inadequate site drainage design or from causes outside the contractors' control.
How fault should be allocated among the general contractor, the excavation subcontractor, and the design professional.
The reasonable cost to repair the property and the diminution in its value.
Whether the mechanic's lien is valid and enforceable and whether any claims are barred by the statute of repose.

timeline.event: Summit Outfitters Co. reached substantial completion of a custom single-family home.
Roe discovered framing and roof structural deficiencies and began investigating the cause.
Complaint filed in Bath Superior Court; cross-claim and third-party complaint follow.

third_parties.name: Rosa T. Birchwood
third_parties.relevance: Owns adjacent land that floods from site runoff and can testify to the grading and drainage conditions.

third_parties.name: Town Code Enforcement Office
third_parties.relevance: Issued the building permit and conducted the foundation and framing inspections.

third_parties.name: Granite State Credit Union
third_parties.relevance: Funded the construction loan and holds a mortgage on the property; records reflect draws against the work.

exchanges.question: What did you hire the contractor to build, and for how much?
exchanges.answer: We hired  to build a custom single-family home for about $1363566. It was supposed to be our long-term home.

exchanges.question: When did the problems start, and what are they?
exchanges.answer: Around 2024-07-12 we started seeing framing and roof structural deficiencies. It has only gotten worse since.

exchanges.question: What did your experts find caused it?
exchanges.answer: They traced it to inadequate site drainage design. The excavation work and the fill were not done right, and the foundation did not follow the plans.

exchanges.question: What will it take to fix?
exchanges.answer: The repair estimate is around $602549, and that does not count what we have spent living elsewhere while this gets sorted out.

exchanges.question: Who do you think is responsible?
exchanges.answer: The general contractor signed the contract, but the excavator and the architect have to own their part too. I want it sorted out among them, not on my back.

exchanges.question: What outcome do you want?
exchanges.answer: Get the house properly repaired, be made whole for the extra costs, and settle this through their insurers if we can. I will go to trial if I have to.

goals: Recover the full reasonable cost to repair the property, approximately $602549, plus consequential damages.
Allocate fault among the general contractor, the excavation subcontractor, and the design professional.
Preserve and resolve the mechanic's lien dispute on favorable terms.
priorities: A properly repaired, structurally sound home.
Holding each responsible party accountable for its share of the loss.
Resolving the case efficiently through the contractors' insurers where possible.
desired_outcomes: A judgment or settlement funding the full repair scope and consequential damages.
An allocation of liability that does not leave the homeowner bearing the contractors' fault.
non_starters: A resolution that leaves the foundation and water-intrusion defects unrepaired.

constraints.budget: Willing to litigate the lien and liability, but settlement at fair value is preferred.
constraints.timeline: Wants the defects repaired before another wet season and the case trial-ready within roughly eighteen months.

issues.issue: Timeliness under the construction limitation period and statute of repose
sub_questions: Do any claims fall outside the period of repose for design and construction?
issues.client_position: The claims were brought within the limitation and repose periods.

issues.issue: Negligence of the contractor, excavator, and design professional
sub_questions: What standard of care applied to each party?
How is fault apportioned among them?
issues.client_position: Each party breached its standard of care and is liable for its share of the harm.

issues.issue: Validity and enforcement of the mechanic's / materialman's lien
sub_questions: Was the lien perfected within the statutory period?
Should the lien be enforced, reduced, or discharged?
issues.client_position: Disputes the amount and enforceability of the lien against the homeowner's setoff.

issues.issue: Negligent excavation and fill compaction by the subcontractor
sub_questions: Was the site excavated and the structural fill compacted to specification?
Did that work cause the settlement and intrusion?
issues.client_position: Improper excavation and uncompacted fill are a root cause of the defects.

issues.issue: Indemnity and contribution among the defendants
sub_questions: Which party must indemnify or contribute to any judgment?
How does comparative fault govern the allocation?
issues.client_position: Supports an allocation that holds each contractor responsible for its own fault.

expert_opinions.topic: Cause of foundation distress and water intrusion
expert_opinions.opinion: The foundation settlement and intrusion result from inadequate support and drainage, not normal settling.
expert_opinions.basis: Site inspection, crack mapping, and review of the structural plans.

expert_opinions.topic: Condition and maintenance of the property
expert_opinions.opinion: Observed conditions are consistent with deferred maintenance and owner grading rather than original construction.
The structural fill was not compacted to specification, allowing differential settlement.
expert_opinions.basis: Soil borings, compaction testing, and the excavation records.

evidence.title: Change orders and pay applications
evidence.description: Project change orders and the contractor's payment applications.

evidence.title: Mechanic's lien filing
evidence.description: The recorded mechanic's / materialman's lien on the property.

evidence.title: Approved foundation and site plans
evidence.description: The stamped foundation and site/grading plans approved for the project.

evidence.title: Repair estimate and scope of work
evidence.description: Itemized repair estimate and remediation scope.

evidence.title: Construction contract and plans
evidence.description: The executed construction agreement, specifications, and drawings.

amounts.label: Estimated repair cost
amounts.basis: Cost to repair to a code-compliant condition.

amounts.label: Consequential damages
amounts.basis: Temporary housing and mold remediation during repairs.

elements: Defective or nonconforming work by the contractor

causes_of_action.claim: Breach of the implied warranty of workmanlike construction
elements: An implied warranty of workmanlike, habitable construction
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Cost to repair and consequential damages

elements: A duty of reasonable care in construction
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Compensatory damages for the cost of repair and diminution in value

elements: A duty to excavate and compact fill to specification
Causation of the settlement and intrusion
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Compensatory damages attributable to the defective sitework

causes_of_action.claim: Enforcement and discharge of the mechanic's lien
elements: A genuine dispute over the amount owed
The homeowner's setoff for defective work
causes_of_action.relief_sought: A declaration reducing or discharging the lien and an accounting

affirmative_defenses.basis: Claims for design and construction are barred by the period of repose. 14 M.R.S. § 752-A.
The claimed repairs would put the homeowner in a better position than the contract required.

affirmative_defenses.defense: Lack of notice and opportunity to cure
affirmative_defenses.basis: The homeowner failed to provide notice and a reasonable opportunity to cure the alleged defects.

cross_claims.basis: If the general contractor is liable, the excavation subcontractor must indemnify and contribute for its defective sitework. 14 M.R.S. § 156.
Any defect traceable to the design entitles the general contractor to indemnity and contribution from the design professional.

third_party_claims.basis: The design professional's deficient foundation and drainage design caused or contributed to the loss. 14 M.R.S. § 156.

discovery.description: Deposition of the general contractor's principal.
Requests for the plans, pay applications, change orders, and soils reports.
Subpoena to the municipal building inspector for permit and inspection records.
Destructive testing and site inspection of the foundation and fill.

motions.title: Motion for summary judgment on the mechanic's lien
motions.relief: Judgment reducing or discharging the recorded lien.

motions.title: Motion to bifurcate liability and damages
motions.relief: Separate trials on liability and damages.

motions.title: Motion to consolidate
motions.relief: Consolidation of the related lien and defect actions.

docket.entry: Complaint filed against the general contractor and excavation subcontractor.
Answer, cross-claim against the subcontractor, and third-party complaint against the design professional filed.
Scheduling order entered; discovery and expert-disclosure deadlines set.
Order granting destructive testing and site inspection of the foundation.
Expert reports on causation, soils, and repair cost served.
Motion for summary judgment on the mechanic's lien taken under advisement.
Mediation held; discovery continues toward trial.

facts.legal_description: The land and improvements at the subject premises, being the same parcel conveyed by warranty deed, together with all buildings thereon
